blocks.content: After a while they drifted toward the back of the arcade. Found a bench near the wall — not unlike last night's, in the way benches become yours when something happens on one.
They sat.
Not far. Not far at all.

He didn't ask what had happened. He didn't try to shape her afternoon into a problem he could address. He just sat close enough that their shoulders nearly touched and turned a token slowly in his fingers, and the quality of his presence was the kind that didn't require anything from you — the kind that said I'm here without making that a demand.
She looked at him sideways.
He was watching the arcade — the kids at the racing game, the flashing machines, the particular organized chaos of a place that had decided noise was the same as life.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Thank you.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I didn't do anything.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I know.
He turned the token over again.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I wanted to see you today.
He said it with the careful precision of someone who had decided to say a true thing and was saying it exactly.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I didn't have a reason. I don't think I needed one.
She looked at him for a moment.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: No. You didn't.
Something settled between them — not dramatic, just the quiet of two people who have discovered, without ceremony, that they want to be near each other.
She leaned her shoulder into his.
He didn't move.
She felt his breathing, slow and steady, and matched it without meaning to.
The afternoon continued around them.
